does: This paper develops an autonomous red teaming framework that combines large language models with reinforcement learning to generate adaptive, multi-stage cyber attacks against AI-enabled security systems. The framework uses a hierarchical architecture where an LLM-based planner generates strategic attack intents while an RL controller executes tactical decisions, with reward shaping guided by the cyber kill chain. The authors evaluate their approach in a high-fidelity enterprise network simulation and demonstrate that their hybrid LLM-RL method substantially outperforms standalone LLM agents and domain-specific cybersecurity models at sustaining complex, multi-stage attack campaigns against autonomous defenders.
connects: This work extends the intersection of adversarial testing and autonomous cyber defense. It builds on prior research in red teaming methodologies for evaluating security systems while incorporating recent advances in LLM-based reasoning and RL-based tactical planning. The paper responds to emerging challenges in SOAR systems that increasingly deploy autonomous agents for cyber defense, addressing a gap where the adversarial robustness of these systems against adaptive attackers remains largely unexplored. The hierarchical design combining LLM planning with RL execution draws on established frameworks for decomposing complex decision-making problems and extends classical cybersecurity concepts like the kill chain into modern AI-driven evaluation contexts.
enables: This framework opens pathways for systematic evaluation of autonomous cyber defense systems against increasingly sophisticated adaptive adversaries. The work could drive development of more robust SOAR systems by identifying vulnerabilities to multi-stage attacks that single-stage or rule-based defenses might miss. Future research could extend this to adversarial training of defenders using the red teaming loop, develop specialized reward functions for different attacker objectives, or investigate game-theoretic equilibria between autonomous attackers and defenders. The methodology also provides a foundation for evaluating other AI-enabled security systems and could inspire defenses specifically designed to counter hybrid LLM-RL based attacks in enterprise environments.
